q: Walk me through the `URLSession` architecture: sessions, tasks, delegates, and configurations.
a: A `URLSession` is the manager object that owns a configuration and a queue, and it vends tasks that do the actual work. Each task — data, download, or upload — represents a single request and runs asynchronously, reporting progress and completion either through completion handlers or through delegate callbacks. The `URLSessionConfiguration` decides caching, cookie storage, timeouts, headers, and whether the session is default, ephemeral, or background. The key mental model is that the session is long-lived and reusable, tasks are cheap and disposable, and the delegate is where you get fine-grained control like authentication challenges, redirects, and per-byte progress.
d: Under the hood `URLSession` runs on its own background threads and dispatches your completion handlers; delegate methods are invoked on the `delegateQueue` you supply (or a serial background queue by default, never the main queue unless you specify it). A subtle but critical point is that a session created with a delegate retains that delegate strongly until you call `finishTasksAndInvalidate()` or `invalidateAndCancel()` — forgetting this is a classic retain-cycle leak. You cannot mix the convenience completion-handler API and the delegate's data-delivery callbacks for the same task: if you supply a completion handler, `urlSession(_:dataTask:didReceive:)` is not called. Configurations are copied at session-creation time, so mutating a configuration after creating the session has no effect. Tasks start suspended and require an explicit `resume()`. The session multiplexes connections via an internal connection pool honoring `httpMaximumConnectionsPerHost`, and supports HTTP/2 multiplexing where the server allows it.
f: Trap: "Can I share one delegate-based session across my whole app to save resources?" You can, but be careful: a single delegate must then disambiguate callbacks by task, and you cannot invalidate it without tearing down all in-flight work. Most apps use a shared default session for simple calls and dedicated sessions only where delegate control (pinning, background, progress) is required.

q: Compare default, ephemeral, and background `URLSessionConfiguration`. When do you choose each?
a: A default configuration uses the shared URL cache, cookie storage, and credential storage persisted to disk, which is what most everyday requests want. An ephemeral configuration keeps caches, cookies, and credentials only in memory and discards them when the session is released, so it's ideal for private-browsing-style features or handling sensitive data. A background configuration hands the transfer off to a system daemon (`nsurlsessiond`) so downloads and uploads continue even after your app is suspended or terminated. I pick default for normal API traffic, ephemeral when I don't want anything touching disk, and background only for large, long-running transfers that must survive app suspension.
d: Background sessions are fundamentally different: they are out-of-process, only support download and upload tasks (no plain data tasks, no completion-handler convenience methods), and require a unique identifier so the system can reconnect your app to in-flight transfers after relaunch. The configuration flags `isDiscretionary` and `sessionSendsLaunchEvents` control whether the system can defer transfers for power/network optimization and whether it relaunches your app on completion. Ephemeral sessions still use the network stack normally but back all storage with memory, so they're not a security boundary against a compromised device, just an avoidance of persistence. Default sessions share global `HTTPCookieStorage` and `URLCache`, which means cookies set in one default session are visible to another — a common surprise. You should create exactly one session per background identifier; recreating a second session with the same identifier throws.
f: Trap: "Use a background session for all uploads so they're reliable." No — background sessions add real overhead and constraints (file-based bodies only, no in-memory `Data`, discretionary scheduling). For a quick foreground upload while the user waits, a default session is faster and simpler; reserve background for transfers that must outlive app suspension.

q: How do background downloads and uploads work, and how is completion handled when the app isn't running?
a: With a background session the actual transfer is performed by the system's `nsurlsessiond` daemon, so it proceeds while your app is suspended or even after it's been terminated. When transfers finish, the system relaunches your app in the background and calls the app delegate's `handleEventsForBackgroundURLSession` method, giving you a completion handler you must store. You then recreate the session with the same identifier, let its delegate fire the normal didFinish callbacks, and once they've all drained you invoke the saved completion handler on the main thread. That final call tells the system it can snapshot and re-suspend your app.
d: The critical mechanics: you must recreate the session with the identical background identifier on relaunch so the daemon can reattach the outstanding tasks to your new in-process session; the delegate then receives `urlSession(_:downloadTask:didFinishDownloadingTo:)` with a temporary file URL that is valid only inside that callback, so you must move it synchronously. The signal that all enqueued events for a background session have been delivered is `urlSessionDidFinishEvents(forBackgroundURLSession:)`, which is where you should call the stored system completion handler. Uploads in background sessions must use a file URL or stream, never an in-memory `Data` body, because the daemon needs something durable to read from across process boundaries. Transfers marked `isDiscretionary` (the default for uploads) may be delayed by the system for power and Wi-Fi reasons. Errors after the app was killed are reported on relaunch; you can also resume interrupted downloads using `resumeData` if the server supports range requests.
f: Trap: "I'll just stash `didFinishDownloadingTo`'s file URL and copy it later." That URL is a temporary location the system deletes the moment the delegate method returns — you must move or copy the file synchronously within the callback, otherwise it's gone.

q: Distinguish `URLSessionDataTask`, download task, and upload task. Why does the distinction matter?
a: A data task delivers the response body incrementally into memory, which is great for typical JSON API calls but dangerous for large payloads because everything accumulates in RAM. A download task streams the response straight to a temporary file on disk and gives you a file URL, so it's the right choice for big assets like videos or model files. An upload task is built for sending a body — from `Data`, a file, or a stream — and reports upload progress. The distinction matters for memory footprint and for background sessions, which only permit download and upload tasks.
d: Data tasks buffer the entire body unless you adopt the delegate's incremental `didReceive data` callback, and even then you're responsible for assembling it; they cannot run in background sessions. Download tasks write to disk as bytes arrive, so peak memory stays flat regardless of file size, and they uniquely support `cancel(byProducingResumeData:)` to later resume via range requests. Upload tasks differ from a data task with an HTTP body mainly in their progress reporting and their support in background sessions; in a foreground session a `POST` via a data task is perfectly valid. A nuance: for download resume to work the server must honor `Range`/`If-Range` and provide a stable validator like `ETag` or `Last-Modified`, otherwise the resume data is useless. Choosing the wrong task type is a real performance bug — using a data task for a 500 MB download can OOM the app.
f: Trap: "Upload tasks are the only way to send a request body." False — a data task can carry an HTTP body just fine. Upload tasks add progress reporting, file/stream bodies, and background-session support; that's when you actually need them.

q: Give a deep dive on `Codable`: how encoding and decoding actually work, and the role of `CodingKeys`.
a: `Codable` is just `Encodable & Decodable`, and the compiler synthesizes the conformance by walking your stored properties. During decoding, the decoder hands your type a keyed container, and the synthesized `init(from:)` pulls each property out by its coding key; encoding is the mirror image. `CodingKeys` is an enum, usually `String`-backed, that maps your Swift property names to the JSON keys, and it's how you rename fields or omit ones you don't want serialized. If you only need to rename, `CodingKeys` plus the synthesized code is enough; once logic gets custom you write `init(from:)` yourself.
d: The synthesized initializer uses `container(keyedBy:)` and then `decode(_:forKey:)` for non-optionals and `decodeIfPresent(_:forKey:)` for optionals — which is exactly why a missing key on an optional yields `nil` but a missing key on a non-optional throws `keyNotFound`. `JSONDecoder` doesn't see your Swift types; it produces a generic intermediate and your type's `Decodable` logic drives extraction, so the type system, not the JSON, decides what's required. Omitting a case from `CodingKeys` removes that property from both encode and decode, but then the property must have a default value or the synthesized init won't compile. `JSONEncoder` and `JSONDecoder` are value-semantics configuration objects you can tune with `keyEncodingStrategy`, `dateDecodingStrategy`, and `dataDecodingStrategy`. A subtlety: `Codable` keys are ordered by declaration for encoding only if you don't set `outputFormatting`; `.sortedKeys` forces alphabetical, which matters for deterministic payloads and signatures.
f: Trap: "A missing JSON key always crashes decoding." Only for non-optional properties. Optionals decode to `nil` via `decodeIfPresent`, and properties with defaults can be made resilient if you write a custom `init(from:)` that falls back. The default synthesized init does NOT apply property default values for missing keys.

q: How do you decode polymorphic or heterogeneous JSON — e.g. an array of items with a `type` discriminator?
a: The standard approach is to first decode a small discriminator field, usually a `type` string, then branch and decode the concrete type into an enum with associated values. You write a custom `init(from:)` that reads the discriminator from a keyed container, switches on it, and decodes the appropriate payload. For arrays of mixed types you decode into `[Wrapper]` where the wrapper holds that enum. The key idea is that `Codable` can't dispatch on runtime type for you, so you encode the type information explicitly and decode it in two passes.
d: Mechanically you typically decode the discriminator from one container, then re-read the same data — either from the same keyed container for inline payloads, or by decoding the whole object into the matched concrete type. A common pattern uses an enum like `enum Item { case photo(Photo), video(Video), unknown }` where the `unknown` case future-proofs against new server types instead of throwing. For arrays you can't easily abort one bad element with the default `[T]` decode because a single failure throws the whole array; the robust technique is a `FailableDecodable` wrapper or manually iterating an `unkeyedContainer()` and catching per-element errors. When you control both ends, a tagged-union representation with a stable discriminator is far more maintainable than trying to detect shape by which keys exist. Beware nested heterogeneous data: you may need `nestedContainer(keyedBy:forKey:)` to descend without flattening your model. Always keep an unknown/default case in production — servers add types, and a hard throw is a needless crash.
f: Trap: "I'll detect the type by checking which optional keys decoded successfully." Fragile and order-dependent — two types sharing keys break it, and it silently mis-decodes. Use an explicit discriminator field and switch on it; if the server lacks one, push for it or key off a guaranteed-present tag.

q: Explain `Codable` date and key strategies, nested containers, and when you'd write a custom `init(from:)`.
a: `JSONDecoder` exposes `dateDecodingStrategy` with options like `.iso8601`, `.secondsSince1970`, `.millisecondsSince1970`, and `.formatted` or `.custom` for anything else, so you rarely parse dates by hand. `keyDecodingStrategy = .convertFromSnakeCase` auto-maps `created_at` to `createdAt` without writing `CodingKeys`. Nested containers let you reach into sub-objects or flatten them into your model using `nestedContainer(keyedBy:forKey:)` and `nestedUnkeyedContainer`. I write a custom `init(from:)` when the JSON shape doesn't match my Swift model one-to-one — flattening, defaults for missing keys, validation, or transforming values during decode.
d: `.convertFromSnakeCase` has a sharp edge: it transforms keys algorithmically, so explicit `CodingKeys` raw values must be written in the already-converted form, and round-tripping with `.convertToSnakeCase` isn't guaranteed lossless for acronyms (`userID` may not map back cleanly). `.iso8601` uses `ISO8601DateFormatter`, which by default does NOT accept fractional seconds — a frequent production bug; you switch to `.custom` with a formatter that includes `.withFractionalSeconds`. Nested containers are the tool for impedance mismatch: you can hoist a deeply nested field up to a top-level property in `init(from:)` without polluting your model with wrapper structs. Custom `init(from:)` is also where you apply defaults — decode with `decodeIfPresent` and coalesce — and where you do cross-field validation, throwing `DataCorruptedError` with context for good error messages. Remember that once you write a custom `init(from:)` you usually owe a matching `encode(to:)` for symmetry. Strategies are decoder-wide, so mixing date formats across a single payload forces per-property handling instead.
f: Trap: "`.iso8601` handles all ISO timestamps." It rejects fractional seconds (e.g. `2026-06-05T12:00:00.123Z`) by default and throws. Use a `.custom` strategy with an `ISO8601DateFormatter` configured `.withInternetDateTime` plus `.withFractionalSeconds`, or you'll get intermittent decode failures.

q: How do you robustly handle missing, null, or dynamically-shaped JSON in production?
a: I make every field whose presence isn't contractually guaranteed an optional, and I use defaults via a custom `init(from:)` so a missing key degrades gracefully instead of crashing. For values that can be either a string or a number, I write a small decoding helper that tries multiple representations. For truly dynamic regions of the payload I decode into an `AnyCodable`-style box or into `[String: JSONValue]` rather than forcing a rigid schema. The guiding principle is to be liberal in what I accept so a server tweak doesn't take down the client.
d: The distinction between missing (`keyNotFound`), null (`valueNotFound` for non-optionals, `nil` for optionals), and wrong-type (`typeMismatch`) matters because each throws a different `DecodingError`, and you handle them differently. A resilient model decodes containers defensively: `try? container.decodeIfPresent(...)` swallows errors at the field level so one bad field doesn't fail the whole object, but you should log those failures rather than silently lose data. For arrays where individual elements may be malformed, a `FailableDecodable<T>` wrapper lets you decode `[FailableDecodable<Item>]` and `compactMap` the successes — partial success instead of total failure. Dynamic keys (like a map of IDs to objects) require a custom `CodingKey` type that accepts any string via `init(stringValue:)`. Be careful that over-permissive decoding hides real bugs; the senior balance is graceful degradation in production paired with strict decoding plus assertions in debug builds. Tools like a typed `JSONValue` enum give you a safe, exhaustive way to walk unknown structure without `Any`.
f: Trap: "Make everything optional and you're safe." Optionals stop crashes but silently mask schema drift and produce subtly broken UI with `nil` everywhere. Pair optionals with defaults, per-field error logging, and contract tests so you notice when the server actually changed rather than just papering over it.

q: Compare REST and GraphQL. What are the real trade-offs for a mobile client?
a: REST gives you resource-oriented endpoints with predictable URLs, strong HTTP caching, and dead-simple debugging, but it often leads to over-fetching or chatty round-trips when a screen needs data from several resources. GraphQL lets the client request exactly the fields it needs in a single round-trip, which is fantastic for complex mobile screens and avoids version churn, but it sacrifices easy HTTP caching and adds client complexity. For mobile specifically, GraphQL's single-request, no-over-fetch model saves bandwidth and battery, while REST's HTTP-level caching and CDN friendliness are hard to beat for static or shared content. I choose based on data shape: deeply relational, screen-specific data leans GraphQL; cacheable resource CRUD leans REST.
d: GraphQL collapses many REST round-trips into one POST, which is a battery and latency win on cellular, but because it's almost always a `POST` to a single endpoint you lose `URLCache`, CDN caching, and `ETag`-based 304s for free — you have to rebuild caching at the client with normalized stores like Apollo's cache. Error semantics differ: GraphQL can return a `200` with a partial `errors` array, so you can't rely on HTTP status alone and must inspect the body. REST's statelessness and uniform interface make observability, retries, and idempotency reasoning straightforward, whereas GraphQL queries are opaque to intermediaries. GraphQL's typed schema and introspection give excellent codegen and eliminate endpoint versioning, but persisted queries are often needed to control payload size and prevent arbitrary expensive queries. N+1 problems move to the server resolver layer. Subscriptions add real-time but bring WebSocket lifecycle complexity to the client. Neither is strictly better; it's about caching needs versus query flexibility.
f: Trap: "GraphQL has no caching." It has no free HTTP caching, but normalized client caches (Apollo/Relay) and persisted-query CDN caching exist. The honest statement is that GraphQL trades transparent HTTP/CDN caching for app-level normalized caching you must configure.

q: Explain HTTP caching on iOS: `URLCache`, `Cache-Control`, `ETag`/`If-None-Match`, and 304s.
a: `URLSession` ships with a `URLCache` that transparently stores responses based on the server's caching headers, so a well-configured backend gets you caching almost for free. `Cache-Control` tells the cache how long a response is fresh and whether it may be stored at all, and once it's stale the client can revalidate. Revalidation uses validators: the server sends an `ETag`, the client sends it back as `If-None-Match`, and if nothing changed the server replies `304 Not Modified` with no body, saving bandwidth. On iOS this all happens inside `URLSession` when the configuration's `requestCachePolicy` allows it, and you can tune or override it per request.
d: `URLCache` keys on the request and respects `Cache-Control` directives like `max-age`, `no-cache` (store but always revalidate), `no-store` (never store), and `private`/`public`. The default `requestCachePolicy` is `.useProtocolCachePolicy`, meaning the HTTP semantics win; you only reach for `.reloadIgnoringLocalCacheData` or `.returnCacheDataDontLoad` for explicit offline behavior. When a cached response goes stale, `URLSession` automatically issues a conditional request with `If-None-Match` (from `ETag`) or `If-Modified-Since` (from `Last-Modified`), and a `304` causes the session to serve the stored body to you transparently — your completion handler sees the full response, not the 304. Importantly, `URLCache` only caches responses up to a size relative to the cache capacity (historically ~5% of disk capacity per item) and skips bodies it deems too large. HTTPS responses are cached in memory by default but only persisted to disk if you opt in, for privacy. You can also implement `willCacheResponse` in the delegate to mutate or veto what gets stored. A common gotcha: `POST` responses are generally not cached.
f: Trap: "`no-cache` means don't cache." It means store the response but always revalidate before reuse; `no-store` is the directive that forbids storing entirely. Conflating them either wastes a revalidation round-trip or wrongly persists sensitive data.

q: How do you implement retry with exponential backoff and jitter, and why is jitter necessary?
a: On a retriable failure I wait a base delay, then double it on each subsequent attempt up to a cap, and I add randomness — jitter — to that delay. Exponential growth keeps a struggling server from being hammered, while jitter spreads retries from many clients so they don't all wake up at the same instant and re-stampede the server. I also bound the total attempts and only retry idempotent or explicitly-safe operations. The classic formula is something like `min(cap, base * 2^attempt)` then multiplied or offset by a random factor.
d: Without jitter, a fleet of clients that all failed at the same moment (say during a deploy) will all retry at exactly `base * 2^n`, producing synchronized thundering-herd spikes that re-crash the recovering service. Full jitter — `random(0, min(cap, base * 2^attempt))` — is the AWS-recommended variant and generally outperforms equal or decorrelated jitter in spreading load. You should distinguish retriable conditions (timeouts, `5xx`, `429`, connection drops) from non-retriable ones (`4xx` like `400`/`401`/`404`), and honor a `Retry-After` header when present instead of your own backoff. Retries should respect cancellation and an overall deadline so you don't keep a spinner alive forever. On iOS, also consider `waitsForConnectivity` on the configuration, which lets `URLSession` itself wait for a usable network rather than failing immediately, reducing the need for naive retries. Crucially, backoff must be paired with idempotency guarantees, or retries can duplicate side effects.
f: Trap: "Just retry 3 times immediately on any error." Immediate, jitter-less retries on non-idempotent calls cause duplicate writes and synchronized load spikes, and retrying `4xx` is pointless. Retry only retriable statuses, with capped exponential backoff plus jitter, honoring `Retry-After` and idempotency keys.

q: What is idempotency in HTTP, and how do you make retries safe for non-idempotent operations?
a: An idempotent operation produces the same server state whether you call it once or many times — `GET`, `PUT`, and `DELETE` are defined as idempotent, while `POST` generally is not. That matters because a retry after a timeout might actually have succeeded on the server, so retrying a `POST` could create a duplicate charge or order. To make non-idempotent operations safe to retry, I attach a client-generated idempotency key, typically a UUID, that the server uses to deduplicate: the first request does the work and records the key, and any retry with the same key returns the original result instead of acting again. This turns an unsafe retry into a safe one.
d: The crux is the ambiguous-failure case: a network timeout tells you nothing about whether the server processed the request, so without idempotency you cannot safely retry a mutation. An idempotency key is stored server-side, usually with the response, for some retention window; the server must atomically check-and-set the key to avoid a race where two concurrent retries both think they're first. Idempotency is about end-state, not response equality — a `DELETE` is idempotent even though the second call returns `404`, because the resource is gone either way. `PUT` (full replace) is naturally idempotent while `PATCH` may not be, depending on whether the patch is absolute or relative. On the client you generate the key once per logical operation and reuse it across all retries of that operation, never regenerating per attempt. Stripe's API is the canonical real-world example of header-based idempotency keys for payments.
f: Trap: "`POST` can never be retried safely." It can — with a stable idempotency key generated once per logical operation and honored server-side for dedup. The mistake is generating a fresh key on each retry, which defeats the entire mechanism.

q: How do you implement auth token refresh with single-flight de-duplication to avoid a refresh stampede?
a: When a request gets a `401`, it should trigger a token refresh, but if ten requests fail at once you don't want ten concurrent refreshes invalidating each other. The fix is single-flight: the first `401` starts the refresh and every other request that needs a token awaits that same in-progress refresh rather than launching its own. Once the refresh completes, all waiters get the new token and retry their original requests. I usually implement this with an actor or a serial queue guarding a shared refresh task.
d: The classic implementation holds an optional in-progress `Task<Token, Error>`; callers check it under a lock or actor, and if one exists they `await` it, otherwise they create it, store it, and clear it on completion. Using a Swift `actor` makes this clean because the check-and-set is naturally serialized without manual locks. Refresh tokens are often single-use and rotated, so a stampede of parallel refreshes can race and invalidate each other, logging the user out — exactly what single-flight prevents. You must also guard against an infinite loop: a refreshed token that still yields `401` should fail rather than retry forever, so cap retries to one post-refresh attempt. Storing the refresh and access tokens belongs in the Keychain, not `UserDefaults`. A further subtlety is request replay: queued requests should be retried with the new token transparently, ideally via a custom `URLProtocol` or an interceptor layer so call sites don't each reimplement it. Clock skew and proactive refresh (refreshing slightly before expiry using `expires_in`) reduce the number of reactive `401`s entirely.
f: Trap: "Just refresh the token on every `401` independently." With rotating refresh tokens, concurrent refreshes race and one invalidates the others, force-logging the user out. Coalesce all concurrent refreshes into a single in-flight task and have the rest await it.

q: Explain certificate pinning: how and where you implement it, and the real risks of pinning.
a: Certificate pinning means the app validates that the server's certificate or public key matches a known good value, beyond the OS's normal trust-store check, to defend against rogue or compromised certificate authorities. On iOS you implement it in the `URLSession` delegate's `didReceive challenge` method for the server-trust authentication space, where you inspect the `SecTrust` chain and compare a hash of the leaf or intermediate public key against pins you ship. You typically pin the public key rather than the whole certificate so rotation is easier, and you ship a backup pin. The big risk is that if your pinned key rotates and you didn't ship the new pin, every client bricks until they update.
d: Mechanically you evaluate the server trust with `SecTrustEvaluateWithError` first (never skip standard validation), then extract the certificate or public key via the Security framework, hash the SubjectPublicKeyInfo, and compare against your pin set, calling the completion handler with `.useCredential` on match or `.cancelAuthenticationChallenge` otherwise. Pinning the public-key SPKI rather than the certificate bytes survives certificate renewal as long as the key is reused, and you should always include at least one backup pin tied to a key held in reserve. The dominant risk is operational, not technical: a pin mismatch is unrecoverable without an app update, so a botched server rotation can take the whole installed base offline — this is why some teams ship pins via a remotely-updatable, signed config. iOS also offers declarative pinning via `NSPinnedDomains` in the `Info.plist` ATS configuration, which is less error-prone than hand-rolled delegate code. Pinning does not protect against a compromised client or a malicious app build, and it complicates debugging proxies. Pin the intermediate CA as a middle-ground that allows leaf rotation while still narrowing trust.
f: Trap: "Pin the leaf certificate for maximum security." Leaf certs rotate frequently (often every 90 days with modern CAs), so leaf pinning guarantees recurring outages. Pin the public key (SPKI) or an intermediate, and always ship a backup pin so rotation doesn't brick clients.

q: What is App Transport Security (ATS), and how do you reason about exceptions?
a: ATS is the iOS default that forces network connections to use HTTPS with TLS 1.2 or higher and forward secrecy, blocking cleartext and weak-crypto connections unless you explicitly opt out. It's configured in the app's `Info.plist`, and Apple wants any exceptions to be narrowly scoped and justified, especially for App Store review. If I must talk to a legacy server I add a domain-specific exception rather than a blanket `NSAllowsArbitraryLoads`, and I document why. The goal is secure-by-default with surgical, justified holes only where unavoidable.
d: ATS applies to `URLSession`, `URLConnection`, and most higher-level networking but notably not to `WKWebView` content the same way, and lower-level `Network.framework` sockets aren't covered by ATS at all. Per-domain exceptions live under `NSExceptionDomains`, where you can relax `NSExceptionMinimumTLSVersion`, allow insecure HTTP via `NSExceptionAllowsInsecureHTTPLoads`, or include subdomains. The blanket `NSAllowsArbitraryLoads` disables ATS globally and draws App Store scrutiny; the more defensible patterns are `NSAllowsArbitraryLoadsInWebContent` (for arbitrary web views) and `NSAllowsLocalNetworking` (for local/dev). ATS also enforces certificate requirements like a minimum key size and SHA-256 signatures, so even an HTTPS endpoint with a weak certificate gets blocked. The forward-secrecy requirement restricts the allowed cipher suites to ECDHE-based ones. Notably, ATS is a client-side policy and not a substitute for pinning — it trusts the system CA store, which pinning further constrains. The recently emphasized `NSPinnedDomains` key integrates pinning declaratively under ATS.
f: Trap: "Set `NSAllowsArbitraryLoads` to true and ATS problems go away." It disables protection app-wide, invites App Store rejection, and is rarely justifiable. Use a scoped `NSExceptionDomains` entry for the specific legacy host, relaxing only the minimum necessary directive.

q: How do you implement a multipart/form-data upload correctly?
a: A multipart upload builds a request body with a unique boundary string separating each part, where each part has its own headers — like `Content-Disposition` naming the field and `Content-Type` for binary parts — followed by the raw bytes. You set the request's `Content-Type` to `multipart/form-data; boundary=...` using that same boundary, then send the assembled body via an upload task. For large files you write the body to a temporary file and upload from that URL so you don't hold everything in memory. The trickiest details are getting CRLF line endings exactly right and terminating with the closing boundary.
d: The wire format is strict: every line uses CRLF (`\r\n`), each part starts with `--boundary`, the final delimiter is `--boundary--`, and a blank line separates a part's headers from its data. The boundary must be a string that cannot appear in the payload, so people generate it from a UUID. Building the body in memory is fine for small forms but fatal for large media; the correct approach streams parts into a file on disk and uses `uploadTask(with:fromFile:)`, which also enables background-session uploads. Forgetting the `Content-Length` is usually fine because `URLSession` computes it, but a malformed boundary or missing closing delimiter causes server-side parse failures that look like flaky uploads. You should set per-part `Content-Type` for binary fields (e.g. `image/jpeg`) and omit it for plain text fields. For very large uploads, prefer the server's resumable/chunked protocol over a single giant multipart body. Don't `String`-concatenate binary data; assemble `Data` directly to avoid encoding corruption.
f: Trap: "Build the multipart body as a `String` then convert to `Data`." Binary file bytes aren't valid UTF-8 and get corrupted by string conversion. Assemble the body as `Data` directly — append header strings encoded as UTF-8, then append the raw file `Data`, then the CRLF and boundaries.

q: How do request prioritization and cancellation work in `URLSession`, and why do they matter for UX?
a: Each task has a `priority` property between 0 and 1 that hints to `URLSession` how to order work, which is useful when, say, the visible image should load before prefetched ones. Cancellation is explicit: you call `cancel()` on a task, which fails it with a cancellation error, and you should cancel work tied to UI that's gone — like a cell that scrolled off or a screen that was dismissed. With async/await, cancelling the surrounding `Task` propagates to the `URLSession` call, so structured concurrency makes cancellation natural. Getting this right keeps the app responsive and avoids wasting bandwidth on results nobody will see.
d: Task `priority` is a hint, not a guarantee, and primarily influences HTTP/2 stream prioritization and the order the session schedules connections; the constants `URLSessionTask.highPriority`, `.defaultPriority`, and `.lowPriority` map to 0.75, 0.5, and 0.25. Cancellation surfaces as `URLError.cancelled` (code -999), and you must handle it distinctly from real failures so you don't show an error UI for an intentional cancel. With the async API, `URLSession.data(for:)` checks cancellation, so wrapping calls in a child `Task` and cancelling it (e.g. in `onDisappear` or a cell's `prepareForReuse`) cleanly aborts the network work. Download tasks support `cancel(byProducingResumeData:)` so a cancel isn't necessarily wasted bytes. A subtle UX bug is the stale-completion race: a cancelled-and-replaced request whose old completion still fires can overwrite newer data, so you must guard with request identity or rely on structured cancellation. Coalescing duplicate in-flight requests for the same resource also matters in list-heavy screens.
f: Trap: "Setting `task.priority = 1.0` forces it to run first." Priority is only a scheduling hint, strongest within HTTP/2 multiplexing on one connection; it won't preempt or guarantee ordering across hosts. For hard ordering, control when you start tasks rather than relying on priority.

q: Describe the Core Data stack: persistent container, coordinator, contexts, and the model.
a: The modern entry point is `NSPersistentContainer`, which bundles together the managed object model, the persistent store coordinator, and a main-queue view context. The managed object model is your schema — entities, attributes, relationships — usually defined in a `.xcdatamodeld`. The persistent store coordinator mediates between contexts and the actual SQLite store on disk, and the `NSManagedObjectContext` is the in-memory scratchpad where you create, fetch, and mutate objects before saving. You typically use the container's `viewContext` for UI work and spin up background contexts for heavy writes.
d: `NSPersistentContainer` was introduced to hide the historically painful manual stack assembly; it loads stores asynchronously via `loadPersistentStores` and configures a `viewContext` pinned to the main queue. The `NSManagedObjectModel` is compiled from the data model and is immutable at runtime; it defines the entity graph the coordinator maps to the store. The `NSPersistentStoreCoordinator` owns one or more persistent stores (SQLite by default, but also binary or in-memory) and serializes access to them. A context tracks object changes, maintains uniquing so the same row maps to one managed object instance within that context, and pushes changes to the coordinator on `save()`. Contexts can be layered with parent/child relationships, where a child's save pushes into its parent's memory rather than to disk. The container also exposes `newBackgroundContext()` and `performBackgroundTask` for off-main work. A key invariant is that each context is tied to a concurrency type and must be used only on its own queue.
f: Trap: "Saving the `viewContext` writes straight to disk every time." A standalone `viewContext` does persist to the store via the coordinator, but a child context's save only pushes changes up to its parent in memory — you must also save the parent (ultimately the store-connected context) for data to actually hit disk.

q: Explain Core Data concurrency: main vs private queue contexts, `perform`, and the no-crossing rule.
a: Core Data contexts are not thread-safe; each one has a concurrency type and you must touch it only on its designated queue. A main-queue context is for the UI, and a private-queue context runs its work on a background queue, which you access through `perform` or `performAndWait` so the framework dispatches your block onto the correct queue. The cardinal rule is that you never pass `NSManagedObject` instances between contexts or threads — you pass `NSManagedObjectID`s and re-fetch on the other side. Violating that leads to subtle, hard-to-reproduce crashes and data corruption.
d: `perform` is asynchronous and `performAndWait` is synchronous, but both guarantee your closure runs on the context's queue; for a private-queue context that's a background queue Core Data manages, and for a main-queue context it's the main thread. Managed objects are bound to the context that fetched them and to that context's queue, so reading even a single property of an object from the wrong thread is undefined behavior — the safe currency between contexts is `NSManagedObjectID`, which is thread-safe and lets the destination context fault the object in. You enable the Core Data concurrency debug flag (`-com.apple.CoreData.ConcurrencyDebug 1`) to crash immediately on violations during development. Saving a background context doesn't automatically refresh the `viewContext`; you either set `automaticallyMergesChangesFromParent` or observe `NSManagedObjectContextDidSave` and call `mergeChanges`. Temporary object IDs become permanent only after save (or `obtainPermanentIDs`), which matters if you hand an ID to another context before saving. Parent/child contexts let a child do work and push to a private parent, keeping the main thread free.
f: Trap: "I'll fetch on a background context and hand the resulting objects to the main thread to display." That crosses contexts and threads — undefined behavior. Pass the `objectID`s to the `viewContext` and re-fetch (or use `object(with:)`) there before reading anything.

q: What is faulting in Core Data, and how does it relate to the managed object lifecycle?
a: A fault is a placeholder managed object whose data hasn't been loaded from the store yet — Core Data gives you the object shell and only fires the fault, pulling the real values, when you first access a property. This lazy loading keeps memory low and makes fetches cheap because you don't hydrate an entire object graph up front. Relationships are also faulted, so traversing them triggers additional fetches on demand. Understanding faulting is essential for both performance tuning and avoiding surprises like accessing a fault after its context is gone.
d: When you fetch, Core Data by default returns managed objects but may keep their attributes as faults, firing them on first property access via a round-trip to the row cache or the store. Firing many relationship faults one-by-one is the Core Data version of an N+1 problem; you mitigate it with `relationshipKeyPathsForPrefetching` or by setting `returnsObjectsAsFaults = false` when you know you'll use the data. The row cache lets a fault fire from memory without hitting SQLite if the row was recently loaded. A deleted or invalidated object becomes a fault that throws when fired, and firing a fault whose context has been deallocated or whose store is unreachable raises an exception — a classic crash. You can deliberately turn an object back into a fault with `refresh(_:mergeChanges:)` to free its memory or to discard unsaved changes. `NSFetchRequest` options like `includesPropertyValues = false` return ID-only faults, useful for batch deletes. Faulting interplays with uniquing: within a context, two fetches for the same row yield the same instance.
f: Trap: "Accessing a property on a fetched object is always cheap." If the object is still a fault, that access triggers a store round-trip; do it inside a loop over many objects and you've got an N+1 storm. Prefetch relationships or set `returnsObjectsAsFaults = false` when you'll need the values.

q: How do you optimize Core Data performance: batching, fetch limits, and `NSFetchedResultsController`?
a: The biggest wins come from not loading more than you need: set `fetchLimit` and `fetchBatchSize` so the store streams results in chunks instead of materializing thousands of objects at once. For bulk writes or deletes, `NSBatchInsertRequest`, `NSBatchUpdateRequest`, and `NSBatchDeleteRequest` operate directly at the SQL level without loading objects into memory. For table and collection views, `NSFetchedResultsController` is the standard tool — it efficiently fetches, sections, and observes changes, driving incremental UI updates. Together these keep memory flat and the main thread responsive even with large datasets.
d: `fetchBatchSize` is the underappreciated lever: it makes the fetch return a special faulting array that loads rows in batches as you scroll, so a 50,000-row table stays cheap. Batch operations bypass the context and the validation/notification machinery, which is exactly why they're fast but also why they don't update in-memory objects — you must reset or refresh contexts afterward, and batch deletes should specify a result type so you can merge the deleted IDs into live contexts. `NSFetchedResultsController` caches its results (give it a `cacheName`) and emits fine-grained delegate callbacks for insert/delete/move/update, which you translate into batch table updates or a diffable snapshot; it must run on the context's queue, usually the `viewContext`. Use `propertiesToFetch` with a dictionary result type to fetch only needed columns. Indexing the predicate's and sort's attributes in the model is essential, otherwise SQLite does full scans. Heavy imports belong on a background context with periodic saves and `reset()` to drop the change-tracking overhead.
f: Trap: "`NSBatchDeleteRequest` is the fast, clean way to delete, period." It deletes at the SQL layer and leaves your in-memory contexts (and any `NSFetchedResultsController`) unaware, showing stale objects until you merge the returned object IDs via `mergeChanges(fromRemoteContextSave:)` or reset the context.

q: Explain Core Data migrations: lightweight vs heavyweight, and when you need a mapping model.
a: When your data model changes, Core Data has to migrate the existing store to the new schema. Lightweight migration is automatic and handles simple, inferable changes — adding or removing attributes, renaming with a hint, adding entities — by deducing a mapping for you. Heavyweight migration is for transformations Core Data can't infer, like splitting an entity or transforming data values, and it requires you to author a mapping model and often a custom `NSEntityMigrationPolicy`. I always prefer lightweight where possible and use versioned models with staged migrations for anything complex.
d: Lightweight migration is enabled with `shouldMigrateStoreAutomatically` and `shouldInferMappingModelAutomatically` (both default true on `NSPersistentContainer`), and it works only when Core Data can infer a unique mapping; renames need a renaming identifier set on the attribute in the model editor. A mapping model (`.xcmappingmodel`) explicitly describes source-to-destination entity and attribute mappings and is required when inference fails — for example transforming a string into a relationship, merging two entities, or computing a new value from old ones. Custom `NSEntityMigrationPolicy` subclasses let you run code per instance during heavyweight migration, but they load source and destination into memory, which can blow up for large stores; staged/progressive migration (migrating across intermediate model versions one hop at a time) keeps memory bounded and avoids needing a mapping model between every pair of versions. Migrations are blocking and can take noticeable time, so you migrate off the main thread and show progress for big stores. iOS 17's `NSStagedMigrationManager` formalizes progressive migrations. Always keep every shipped model version in the bundle, since users may upgrade across several at once.
f: Trap: "Lightweight migration can rename an attribute automatically." Only if you set the renaming identifier on the destination attribute pointing at the old name — otherwise Core Data sees a delete plus an add and you lose the data. Inference handles add/remove, not silent renames.

q: Compare SwiftData and Core Data. When would you choose one over the other today?
a: SwiftData is Apple's newer, Swift-native persistence framework built on top of the same Core Data engine, using macros like `@Model` and the `@Query` property wrapper to integrate cleanly with SwiftUI. It's far more concise — no `.xcdatamodeld` editor, no boilerplate stack — and feels natural in a SwiftUI app. Core Data is the mature, battle-tested option with finer-grained control, richer migration tooling, and broader platform-version support. Today I reach for SwiftData on new SwiftUI apps targeting recent OS versions, and stick with Core Data when I need deep control, older OS support, or features SwiftData hasn't fully matched yet.
d: SwiftData models are plain Swift classes annotated `@Model`; the macro generates the underlying schema and observation, and `ModelContainer`/`ModelContext` mirror `NSPersistentContainer`/`NSManagedObjectContext`. Because it sits atop Core Data, the two can interoperate to a degree, sharing a store, which eases incremental adoption. SwiftData embraces value-like ergonomics and automatic change tracking, with `@Query` re-running fetches reactively in SwiftUI, but it abstracts away things you sometimes need: custom migration policies, fine concurrency control, and complex fetch tuning are less exposed (improving with each release, e.g. richer migration plans and history tracking). Concurrency in SwiftData uses `ModelActor` to safely operate off the main thread, analogous to private-queue contexts. SwiftData requires newer minimum OS versions, which is often the deciding constraint for production apps with a wide install base. For apps with intricate data needs, heavyweight migrations, or precise performance control, Core Data remains the safer bet; for greenfield SwiftUI apps, SwiftData drastically cuts boilerplate.
f: Trap: "SwiftData replaces Core Data, so I should migrate my big production app to it." SwiftData is built on Core Data, not a wholesale replacement, and still lacks some advanced control and broad OS support. Adopt it for new SwiftUI work or incrementally; don't rip out a complex Core Data stack for parity features you'd lose.

q: Where would you store secrets vs preferences vs files: Keychain, `UserDefaults`, files, and data protection?
a: Secrets — tokens, passwords, keys — go in the Keychain, which is encrypted and backed by the Secure Enclave-protected key hierarchy, with accessibility classes controlling when they're readable. Small, non-sensitive preferences like a theme choice or a feature flag go in `UserDefaults`, which is a plist and absolutely not secure. Larger structured or binary data goes in files in the appropriate sandbox directory, where you apply data protection classes so the OS encrypts them based on device-lock state. The rule of thumb is: secrets in Keychain, lightweight settings in `UserDefaults`, everything bigger in files with the right protection.
d: `UserDefaults` is an unencrypted plist in the app container, readable from backups and by anyone with file access, so storing a token there is a real vulnerability; it's also loaded into memory wholesale, so it's wrong for large blobs. The Keychain encrypts items and gates them by accessibility class — `kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlocked`, `...AfterFirstUnlock`, and the `...ThisDeviceOnly` variants that exclude items from backups and migration; `WhenPasscodeSetThisDeviceOnly` requires a device passcode. File data protection classes (`NSFileProtectionComplete`, `CompleteUnlessOpen`, `CompleteUntilFirstUserAuthentication`, `None`) map to per-file encryption keys wrapped by the device passcode, so `Complete` files are unreadable while the device is locked — important for background work that may run locked. The Keychain survives app uninstall (a known gotcha for tokens), whereas the app container is wiped. For background `URLSession` writes you often need `CompleteUntilFirstUserAuthentication` so the daemon can write while locked after first unlock. Choose `ThisDeviceOnly` accessibility for secrets that should never sync to a new device via iCloud Keychain or encrypted backup.
f: Trap: "I'll just store the auth token in `UserDefaults` since it's convenient." `UserDefaults` is an unencrypted plist included in backups — that's a credential leak. Tokens belong in the Keychain with an appropriate accessibility class like `kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly`.

q: How do you handle write conflicts in Core Data — optimistic concurrency and merge policies?
a: Core Data uses optimistic concurrency: it doesn't lock rows, it detects conflicts at save time by comparing snapshot versions, and if two contexts changed the same object you get a merge conflict. You resolve these with a context's `mergePolicy`, choosing whether the in-memory version wins, the store version wins, or you merge property-by-property. The default `NSErrorMergePolicy` throws so you're forced to decide, while policies like `NSMergeByPropertyObjectTrumpMergePolicy` automatically favor in-memory changes. Picking the right policy is a domain decision about which writer should win.
d: Each managed object carries a snapshot of the values it was last fetched/saved with; on save, Core Data checks the persistent store's current row state against that snapshot, and a mismatch produces an `NSMergeConflict`. The built-in policies are `NSErrorMergePolicy` (fail and surface conflicts for manual handling), `NSMergeByPropertyStoreTrumpMergePolicy` (store wins per conflicting property), `NSMergeByPropertyObjectTrumpMergePolicy` (in-memory wins per property), `NSOverwriteMergePolicy` (force in-memory whole-object), and `NSRollbackMergePolicy` (discard in-memory). Property-level policies merge non-conflicting changes from both sides and only resolve the actually-conflicting properties, which is usually what you want for collaborative edits. For true correctness you sometimes implement custom conflict resolution by subclassing `NSMergePolicy` and overriding `resolve(constraintConflicts:)`, especially with unique constraints where insert collisions occur. This pairs with `automaticallyMergesChangesFromParent` and persistent history tracking when multiple processes (like an app extension) share a store. The optimistic model assumes conflicts are rare; if they're frequent, reconsider your context architecture.
f: Trap: "Set `NSMergeByPropertyObjectTrumpMergePolicy` and conflicts just disappear." It silently resolves by letting in-memory changes win per property, which can clobber another writer's legitimate update without you noticing. For data where lost updates matter, use `NSErrorMergePolicy` or a custom policy and handle conflicts explicitly.
